ambiguities: cos-lending-selling-dags and cos-lending-selling-ingestion reference 'Vampire system' and 'Vampire DB' but the purpose and API contract of this system are unclear
Cos.Lending.Selling.Contracts defines 'field1' through 'field21' properties on LoanInfo but their business purpose is not documented
Cos.Lending.Selling.Contracts references 'GroomingRecovery' process but implementation details are unclear
Cos.Lending.Selling.Contracts references 'COS transfer system' in AddTransferRequest but the exact nature of this system is unclear
cos-lending-selling-ingestion uses loan status codes (e.g., 'status >= 5') without explanation of their business meaning
cos-lending-selling-ingestion widely uses 'mpl_id' field but the list of valid MPLs is not explicitly defined in code
cos-lending-selling-ingestion references 'Arix' system relationship to 'Selling' system but the relationship is not fully explained

architecturePatterns.description: WebApi uses multiple outbox tables (TransferOutbox, FeeOutbox, NotificationOutbox, etc.) to ensure reliable event publishing to downstream systems. The outbox processors poll these tables and dispatch events via SQS and HTTP, guaranteeing at-least-once delivery semantics.

System is decomposed into focused services: WebApi for business logic, Hooks for notifications, AI for natural language queries, Ingestion for data synchronization, and DAGs for orchestration. Each service owns its domain and communicates via REST APIs and messaging.

System supports multiple tenant types (SellingITAdmin, InternalTenant, MplTenant, BankTenant, InvestorTenant) with application-level tenant filtering applied to database queries. Each user session is scoped to a specific tenant context.

WebApi implements business logic in F# (functional core) for interest calculations, fee processing, and loan operations, while C# handles API layer, infrastructure, and I/O (imperative shell). This separates pure business rules from side effects.

Airflow DAGs orchestrate data movement from source systems (Arix, Contracts, Volume, SOFR) into PostgreSQL warehouse, then transform using DBT. Raw data is loaded first, then transformed in-database for performance.
Cos.Lending.Selling.Contracts defines canonical data types in F# that are shared across all services, ensuring type safety and consistent domain modeling across the distributed system.
Data access is abstracted through repository interfaces, with Entity Framework Core providing ORM capabilities. This allows business logic to remain database-agnostic and facilitates testing.

dataFlows.name: Loan Purchase Flow
dataFlows.description: MPL purchases loans from Cross River Bank through automated or manual workflows, triggering financial transfers and accounting entries.
steps: MPL initiates purchase request via UI or auto-purchase configuration
WebApi validates contract terms and loan eligibility
Batch is created grouping multiple loans for processing
WebApi generates Invoice and writes to BatchInitOutbox
Outbox processor triggers transfer requests to COS Transaction Service
Accounting API is called to retrieve account configurations via data-utils
Financial transfers execute (purchase price from MPL account to bank)
Receipt is generated confirming purchase completion
Hooks service publishes BatchPurchaseCompleted notification
UI displays updated loan ownership and batch status

dataFlows.name: Daily Interest Accrual Flow
dataFlows.description: Automated process calculates and records interest on all active loans daily, with pass-through interest calculations for investors.
steps: Airflow DAG triggers daily interest calculation job
WebApi identifies all active loans requiring interest accrual
F# business logic calculates interest based on loan type, rate, and SOFR data
Interest records written to InterestHistory table
DailyInterestOutbox entries created for each accrual
Outbox processor triggers transfer requests for interest collection
COS Transaction Service executes transfers (interest from MPL to bank)
InterestHistory updated with transfer confirmations
Datatools exports interest data to S3 for reporting
CloudWatch metrics updated with daily interest totals

dataFlows.name: Data Ingestion and Synchronization Flow
dataFlows.description: External data sources are ingested, transformed, and synchronized into the Selling database to maintain current loan and contract information.
steps: Airflow DAG schedules ingestion tasks on regular intervals
Ingestion service fetches SOFR rates from Federal Reserve API
CSV files from MPL servicing systems uploaded to S3
Ingestion service reads S3 files and parses servicing data
Loan status synchronized from Arix database to Selling database
Contract data imported from Contracts database
Volume data imported from Volume database
DBT transformations run to curate and denormalize data
Flyway migrations applied to update schema if needed
Data-utils resolves accounts for newly ingested loans
CloudWatch metrics published for data freshness monitoring

dataFlows.name: Fee Collection Flow
dataFlows.description: Various fees (servicing, volume, true-up) are calculated and collected from MPL accounts according to contract terms.
steps: WebApi identifies fees due based on contract configuration
Volume fee calculation triggered monthly based on loan volume
True-up volume fee calculated if monthly minimum not met
Fee records created with amount, type, and due date
FeeOutbox and VolumeFeeOutbox entries created
Outbox processor calls Accounting API for fee account mapping
Transfer requests generated for fee collection
COS Transaction Service executes transfers (fee from MPL to bank)
Transfer status monitored and updated in database
Hooks service publishes TrueUpVolumeFeeCharged notifications
Reporting exports include fee collection summaries

dataFlows.name: Loan Type Change and Grooming Flow
dataFlows.description: Loans are reclassified or groomed based on seasoning periods and business rules, potentially changing investor allocation.
steps: Airflow DAG triggers grooming job based on loan age
WebApi identifies loans meeting seasoning criteria
Loan type change business logic evaluates eligibility
Investor allocation rules applied based on new loan type
LoanTypeChanged event created with old/new type details
Accounting API called to determine new account mappings
Transfer requests generated if investor change required
Database updated with new loan type and investor
Hooks service publishes LoanTypeChanged notification
Hooks service publishes InvestorChanged notification if applicable
UI reflects updated loan classification

dataFlows.name: AI-Powered Loan Query Flow
dataFlows.description: Users query loan history and details using natural language, with AI generating SQL and synthesizing human-readable responses.
steps: User submits natural language question via UI chat interface
UI calls AI service /chat endpoint to create or resume session
AI service retrieves loan context from PostgreSQL Selling database
User message sent to /chat/{session_id}/message endpoint
AI service constructs prompt with database schema and loan context
AWS Bedrock (Claude) generates SQL query to answer question
AI service validates SQL is SELECT-only and executes against database
Query results formatted and sent back to Claude for summarization
AI streams response via Server-Sent Events to UI
Message history stored in DynamoDB for context retention
UI displays formatted answer with follow-up query capability

dataModel.description: Core entity representing a consumer loan with loan_id, loan_number, mpl_id, issuing_bank_id, servicing_bank_id, investor_id, loan_type, loan_amount, interest_rate, status, origination_date, maturity_date, and sale_date. Tracks the complete loan lifecycle from origination through sale and servicing.

Defines legal and financial terms between Cross River Bank and MPLs, including fee structures (servicing fees, volume fees, interest calculations), seasoning periods, auto-purchase rules, and effective date ranges. Represents the master agreement governing loan transactions.

Audit trail of actions performed on loans including purchases, status changes, interest accruals, fee collections, and loan type changes. Each action has action_type, action_date, amount, and references to related entities. Provides complete history for regulatory compliance.

Represents financial transfers between accounts with transfer_type (purchase, interest, fee), amount, source_account, destination_account, status (pending, completed, failed), and external_transfer_id. Links loan operations to actual money movement via COS Transaction Service.

Financial account entity with account_number, account_type, owner (MPL, Bank, or Investor), and purpose/objective (purchase, return, interest income, interest expense, fee income, fee sweep). Maps loans to specific GL accounts for accounting.

Groups multiple loans for bulk purchase processing with batch_id, mpl_id, batch_date, status, total_amount, loan_count, and progress tracking. Enables atomic multi-loan transactions with rollback capabilities.

Time-series record of interest calculations with loan_id, calculation_date, principal_balance, interest_rate, SOFR_rate (if applicable), daily_interest_amount, and cumulative_interest. Supports pass-through interest to investors and regulatory reporting.

Fee configuration and collection records with fee_type (servicing, volume, origination), rate/amount, collection_frequency, mpl_id, contract_id, and calculation_method. Supports both fixed and percentage-based fees with minimum thresholds.

Entity representing organizations that purchase loan participation interests, with investor_id, name, account configurations, and allocation rules. Investors receive pass-through interest and may have specific loan type preferences.

Marketplace lending platform entity with mpl_id, name, contract references, account configurations, and auto-purchase settings. MPLs originate loans through issuing banks and purchase them through the platform.

Financial institution entity (issuing or servicing banks) with bank_id, name, and account configurations. Issuing banks originate loans on behalf of MPLs, while servicing banks handle ongoing loan administration.

features.name: Automated Loan Purchasing
features.description: MPLs can configure auto-purchase rules to automatically acquire eligible loans based on criteria like loan type, issuing bank, and contract terms. Reduces manual intervention and accelerates loan acquisition.

features.name: Batch Loan Processing
features.description: Enables grouping multiple loans into batches for atomic bulk purchase operations. Provides progress tracking, rollback on failure, and consolidated financial reporting for large transactions.

features.name: Interest Accrual and Pass-Through
features.description: Automated daily calculation of interest on loans using simple or SOFR-based rates. Supports pass-through interest allocation to investors and generates accounting entries for interest income/expense.

features.name: Fee Collection and Management
features.description: Configurable fee structures including servicing fees, volume fees, and origination fees. Automatic calculation and collection with monthly minimums and true-up processing when volume thresholds aren't met.

features.name: Loan Grooming and Seasoning
features.description: Automatic loan reclassification based on aging and seasoning rules defined in contracts. Loans mature through defined periods and may change type or investor allocation based on business rules.
features.dataFlow: Loan Type Change and Grooming Flow

features.name: Natural Language Loan Queries
features.description: AI-powered chat interface allows users to ask questions about loans in natural language. Claude generates SQL queries, retrieves data, and synthesizes human-readable answers about loan history, interest calculations, and transfers.

features.name: Real-Time Event Notifications
features.description: Hooks service publishes notifications for critical events including loan sale status changes, batch completions, investor changes, loan type changes, fee charges, and overdue loan alerts. Enables downstream system integration.

features.name: Loan Inventory Management
features.description: Frontend provides comprehensive views of loan portfolios with filtering, sorting, and searching. Tracks HFS (Held For Sale) loans, pending approvals, and LTHF (Loans To Be Held For Sale) categories.

features.name: Servicing Data Ingestion
features.description: Imports loan servicing data from MPL systems via S3 CSV files. Includes adjusted loan amounts, interest paid, and status updates from external servicing platforms.

features.name: Reconciliation and Reporting
features.description: Generates pre-sale and post-sale reports for regulatory compliance and business intelligence. Exports data to S3 for consumption by downstream analytics systems. Tracks discrepancies between systems.

features.name: Account Resolution and Mapping
features.description: Resolves appropriate GL accounts for loan actions (purchase, return, interest, fees) by querying Accounting API and applying custom mapping rules based on loan attributes.

serviceMap.role: Core business logic and transaction orchestration service. Handles loan purchases, interest accruals, fee processing, batch operations, and transfer management. Implements outbox pattern for reliable event publishing.

React-based frontend providing loan inventory management, search/filtering, approval workflows, and AI-powered chat interface for loan queries. Primary user interface for MPLs, banks, and internal users.

Event notification service that converts domain events into standardized hooks notifications. Publishes loan status changes, batch completions, investor changes, fee charges, and overdue alerts to downstream consumers via NServiceBus.

AI-powered question-answering service using Claude via AWS Bedrock. Enables natural language queries about loan history, interest calculations, and transfers. Generates SQL from user questions and synthesizes human-readable responses.

Data ingestion and synchronization service that imports loan servicing data from S3, SOFR rates from Federal Reserve, and synchronizes loan status between Arix and Selling databases. Publishes CloudWatch metrics.

Airflow-based orchestration service managing ETL workflows for data ingestion, transformation, curation, interest accruals, fee processing, and reporting. Schedules and monitors data pipeline execution.
Data engineering toolkit providing DBT transformations, data exports, and analytics support. Transforms raw ingested data into curated datasets for reporting and business intelligence.
Account resolution utility that maps loans to appropriate GL accounts by querying the Accounting API and applying custom mapping rules. Runs as CLI tool or scheduled job.
Shared database model and Entity Framework context defining schema, entities, relationships, and outbox tables. Provides persistence layer for all services accessing the Selling PostgreSQL database.

Shared F# library defining canonical data types and contracts for loans, accounts, batches, invoices, receipts, and discrepancies. Ensures type safety and consistent domain modeling across all services.

End-to-end testing infrastructure simulating the complete lending environment. Validates loan processing from origination through purchase, including interest, fees, and transfers. Includes mock COS services.

systemOverview: The COS Lending Selling Platform is a comprehensive loan management and secondary market system for Cross River Bank. Its business purpose is to enable Marketplace Lenders (MPLs) to purchase consumer loans originated through issuing banks, manage the complete loan lifecycle including servicing transfers, and handle all associated financial operations. The system orchestrates loan sales from Cross River Bank to MPLs, tracks loan ownership and servicing rights, automates daily interest accruals using simple or SOFR-based calculations, collects various fees (servicing, volume, origination), manages investor relationships for loan participations, and provides regulatory reporting and reconciliation capabilities. The platform supports multi-tenant operations with role-based access for internal administrators, MPLs, issuing banks, servicing banks, and investors. Key workflows include automated batch purchasing of loans, daily interest calculation and pass-through to investors, monthly fee collection with true-up processing, loan grooming and type changes based on seasoning periods, and real-time event notifications to downstream systems. The architecture follows microservices patterns with a React frontend (UI), .NET/F# backend API (WebApi), Python-based AI query service, Airflow data orchestration (DAGs), data ingestion services, and event notification service (Hooks). All services share a common PostgreSQL database schema (DbModel) and type definitions (Contracts). The system integrates with Cross River Bank's core systems (COS Transaction Service, COS Accounting Service, COS Storage Service) for financial operations, external MPL servicing systems via S3 CSV imports, Federal Reserve for SOFR rate data, and AWS services (S3, SQS, DynamoDB, Bedrock) for cloud capabilities.
